acceptedQuestions.subject: Economy
acceptedQuestions.prompt: In the context of economic mobility and trade negotiations between India and the United Kingdom, the 'Double Contribution Convention (DCC)' is primarily intended to:

options.text: Eliminate the requirement for Indian professionals to pay income tax in both countries simultaneously.
Exempt temporary Indian workers and their employers from social security contributions in the UK.
Provide a framework for the mutual recognition of professional qualifications in the healthcare sector.
Facilitate the transfer of accumulated pension funds for Indian citizens permanently migrating to the UK.

acceptedQuestions.explanation: The Double Contribution Convention (DCC) is a specific mechanism in the India-UK economic mobility framework. It exempts Indian workers and their employers from social security contributions in the UK for up to three years when they are on temporary assignments. This prevents workers from paying into a system from which they may not receive benefits due to the short duration of their stay.
acceptedQuestions.takeaway: The DCC is a key tool for reducing the cost of doing business and improving the competitiveness of Indian professionals on temporary assignments abroad.
acceptedQuestions.evidenceExcerpt: Double Contribution Convention (DCC): It exempts Indian workers and their employers from social security contributions in the UK for up to three years when on temporary assignments.
acceptedQuestions.reviewNotes: The question distinguishes between social security (DCC) and income tax (DTAA), which is a common point of confusion in international economic law, making it a good conceptual test.

reviewerNotes: The selected questions cover three distinct pillars of the India-UK relationship: the broad trade/education framework, the technical social security mechanism (DCC), and the institutional investment bridge (UKIIFB). This ensures no overlap while covering the most high-yield facts from the source text. Distractors were refined to be plausible and parallel.
